title: The Armada Gun[1]
content:
В В В В An ancient cannon, finely cast.
В В В В Of bronze, all smooth and green with age,
В В В В A by-gone actor on the stage,
В В В В Yet fit to take, as in the past
В В В В A role in war, and be the last
В В В В Dread argument of kings!

В В В В The daisies grew around, and brought
В В В В The homage of young spring to praise
В В В В This stately relic of old days,
В В В В When France with Spain for mastery fought;
В В В В And Philip over England sought
В В В В To spread the Papal wings.

В В В В Initialed with King Francis' name,
В В В В With Gallic lilies sculptured o'er,
В В В В Above the vent the metal bore
В В В В A Salamander crowned, in flame;
В В В В The massive breech could even claim
В В В В A sheath of lotos bloom.

В В В В This goodly weapon, forged where Seine
В В В В By Fontainebleau and Paris flows,
В В В В And many a painted Palace shows
В В В В These emblems of the Valois' reign,
В В В В For centuries unseen has lain
В В В В Within the sea's dark tomb.

В В В В How came it there? A Spanish keel
В В В В One of the Great Armada gay,
В В В В Was blasted in Our Lady's Bay;
В В В В One of the Fleet the floods conceal,
В В В В Though o'er the waves was wont to peal
В В В В The thunder of their pride.

В В В В But how came France's lilies there
В В В В Beneath the flag of red and gold?
В В В В And o'er the ancient gun we told
В В В В The story which the legends bear,
В В В В How in defeat it bore its share
В В В В And stemmed the Victory's tide.

В В В В We thought the winds of hollow sound
В В В В Spoke from its mouth in solemn tone,
В В В В Of great events its life had known,
В В В В That thronged, as with the nearly drowned,
В В В В To recollection, ere it found
В В В В Beneath the sea a grave.

В В В В "'In flame I live, I quench its glow;'
В В В В This motto at the foundry fire
В В В В Was given me by his desire,
В В В В The king, whose crest and lilies show
В В В В How love and valour could bestow
В В В В Their favour on the brave.

В В В В "My form was fashioned in each part
В В В В By him who wrought in gems and gold,
В В В В Whose glory, trumpet-tongued, is told
В В В В In fearful wars, in peaceful Art,
В В В В Cellini of the ardent heart,
В В В В And Benvenuto named!

В В В В "The silver-voiced and laughing crowd
В В В В Of ladies praised his fair design
В В В В And asked if on the German Rhine,
В В В В Or English coasts of fog and cloud,
В В В В Would soon be heard my challenge loud
В В В В For rights our country claimed?

В В В В "To conquer fair Milan I threw
В В В В My shot against the Swiss array
В В В В On Marignano's dreadful day:
В В В В On sledges hardy soldiers drew
В В В В My weight through snows, where eagles knew
В В В В Alone the Alpine way.

В В В В "And warring for the emperor's crown,
В В В В I saw around me fall and die
В В В В The noblest of our chivalry:
В В В В When peerless Bayard's high renown
В В В В Quenched not his blood, that streaming down
В В В В Fell on me where I lay.

В В В В "Pavia felt my iron hail,
В В В В When traitor Bourbon won the fight,
В В В В Yet glad was I no foreign knight
В В В В Alone had made our siege to fail,
В В В В When wrote our king the dismal tale,
В В В В 'Save honour all is lost!'

В В В В "The impious victor hurled my fire
В В В В Against the walls of holy Rome,
В В В В But there the devil took him home!
В В В В For at the storm my artist sire,
В В В В Cellini, felled him, for the ire
В В В В Of God his path had crossed.

В В В В "To nobler masters still a slave,
В В В В I felt the fame of Doria mine;
В В В В Saw Venice o'er her channels shine;
В В В В Pursued the Moslem on the wave,
В В В В And shattered them, when victory gave
В В В В Her palm to Malta's isle.

В В В В "When Naples sent her ships to swell
В В В В The swarming armaments that bore
В В В В 'Gainst England from each southern shore
В В В В In fleets whose numbers none could tell;
В В В В I saw how Drake upon us fell,
В В В В How fortune ceased to smile.

В В В В "For tempests gathered o'er our track,
В В В В The little English hornets stung,
В В В В My heavy shot against them flung
В В В В Passed o'er their barks, so swift to tack,
В В В В And every ball they gave us back
В В В В Upon our galleons told.

В В В В "Soon drifting o'er the Northern main
В В В В Grey shores unknown were quickly past;
В В В В Our consorts on the rocks were cast,
В В В В It was our fate alone to gain
В В В В The peaceful haven where Maclaine
В В В В Set fire unto our hold.

В В В В I sank: a hundred years past by,
В В В В And diving bells with searchers keen
В В В В For treasure in the wreck were seen.
В В В В They took the gold, but let me lie
В В В В To sleep another century,
В В В В Then raised and brought me here.

В В В В *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *

В В В В "Valois is dead, and Bourbon's Line
В В В В No longer fills my country's throne.
В В В В But death dear France shall never own!
В В В В Once more of late her joy was mine,
В В В В Once more for her my flames could shine,
В В В В My thunder echo clear.

В В В В "For when the tide of battle rolled
В В В В Against the far Crimean shore,
В В В В And France and Britain downward bore
В В В В The Russian in his chosen hold,
В В В В My last salute of victory told
В В В В For France, as oft of yore!"
meaning:
About: John George Edward Henry Douglas Sutherland Campbell, 9th and 2nd Duke of Argyll, usually better known by the courtesy title Marquess of Lorne, by which he was known before 1900, was a British nobleman and was Governor General of Canada from 1878 to 1883. 
He is now remembered primarily for the place names bestowed on Canadian geography in honour of his wife, for his metrical paraphrase of Psalm 121, "Unto the hills around do I lift up" and for the frequency with which the name "Lorne" is given to male children in Canada, a custom uncommon elsewhere.
